title: Catalan language
content:
Catalan (; autonym: or;), known in the Valencian Community and Carche as Valencian, is a Western Romance language derived from Vulgar Latin. It is the official language of Andorra, and a co-official language of three autonomous communities in eastern Spain: Catalonia, the Valencian Community, and the Balearic Islands. It also has semi-official status in the Italian comune of Alghero. It is also spoken in the Pyrénées-Orientales department of France and in two further areas in eastern Spain: the eastern strip of Aragon and the Carche area in the Region of Murcia. The Catalan-speaking territories are often called the or "Catalan Countries".

The language evolved from Vulgar Latin in the Middle Ages around the eastern Pyrenees. Nineteenth-century Spain saw a Catalan literary revival, culminating in the early 1900s. Etymology and pronunciation. The word "Catalan" is derived from the territorial name of Catalonia, itself of disputed etymology. The main theory suggests that (Latin "Gathia Launia") derives from the name "Gothia" or "Gauthia" ("Land of the Goths"), since the origins of the Catalan counts, lords and people were found in the March of Gothia, whence "Gothland" "Gothlandia" "Gothalania" "Catalonia" theoretically derived.

In <a href="English%20language" English</a, the term referring to a person first appears in the mid 14th century as "Catelaner", followed in the 15th century as "Catellain" (from <a href="French%20language" French</a). It is attested a language name since at least 1652. The word "Catalan" can be pronounced in English as, or. The <a href="endonym" endonym</a is pronounced in the Eastern Catalan dialects, and in the Western dialects.

In the <a href="Valencian%20Community" Valencian Community</a and <a href="Carche" Carche</a, the term is frequently used instead. Thus, the name "Valencian", although often employed for referring to the varieties specific to the Valencian Community and Carche, is also used by Valencians as a name for the language as a whole, synonymous with "Catalan".

Both uses of the term have their respective entries in the dictionaries by the <a href="Acad%C3%A8mia%20Valenciana%20de%20la%20Llengua" AVL</a and the <a href="Institut%20d%27Estudis%20Catalans" IEC</a. See also <a href="%23About%20Valencian" status of Valencian</a below. History. Middle Ages.

By the 9th century, Catalan had evolved from <a href="Vulgar%20Latin" Vulgar Latin</a on both sides of the eastern end of the <a href="Pyrenees" Pyrenees</a, as well as the territories of the Roman province of <a href="Hispania%20Tarraconensis" Hispania Tarraconensis</a to the south. From the 8th century onwards the Catalan counts extended their territory southwards and westwards at the expense of the <a href="Muslims" Muslims</a, bringing their language with them.

This process was given definitive impetus with the separation of the <a href="County%20of%20Barcelona" County of Barcelona</a from the <a href="Carolingian%20Empire" Carolingian Empire</a in 988. In the 11th century, documents written in <a href="macaronic%20language" macaronic Latin</a begin to show Catalan elements, with texts written almost completely in Romance appearing by 1080.

Old Catalan shared many features with <a href="Gallo-Romance%20languages" Gallo-Romance</a, diverging from <a href="Old%20Occitan" Old Occitan</a between the 11th and 14th centuries. During the 11th and 12th centuries the Catalan rulers expanded up to north of the <a href="Ebro%20river" Ebro river</a, and in the 13th century they conquered the <a href="Kingdom%20of%20Valencia" Land of Valencia</a and the <a href="Balearic%20Islands" Balearic Islands</a.

The city of <a href="Alghero" Alghero</a in <a href="Sardinia" Sardinia</a was repopulated with Catalan speakers in the 14th century. The language also reached <a href="Murcia" Murcia</a, which became Spanish-speaking in the 15th century. In the <a href="Low%20Middle%20Ages" Low Middle Ages</a, Catalan went through a golden age, reaching a peak of maturity and cultural richness.

Examples include the work of Majorcan polymath <a href="Ramon%20Llull" Ramon Llull</a (1232–1315), the Four Great Chronicles (13th–14th centuries), and the Valencian school of poetry culminating in <a href="Ausi%C3%A0s%20March" Ausiàs March</a (1397–1459).

By the 15th century, the city of <a href="Valencia" Valencia</a had become the sociocultural center of the <a href="Crown%20of%20Aragon" Crown of Aragon</a, and Catalan was present all over the <a href="Mediterranean" Mediterranean</a world. During this period, the Royal Chancery propagated a highly standardized language. Catalan was widely used as an official language in Sicily until the 15th century, and in Sardinia until the 17th. During this period, the language was what Costa Carreras terms "one of the'great languages' of medieval Europe".

<a href="Joanot%20Martorell" Martorell</a's outstanding novel of chivalry "<a href="Tirant%20lo%20Blanc"Tirant lo Blanc</a" (1490) shows a transition from Medieval to Renaissance values, something that can also be seen in <a href="Bernat%20Metge" Metge</a's work. The first book produced with movable type in the <a href="Iberian%20Peninsula" Iberian Peninsula</a was printed in Catalan. Start of the modern era.

With the union of the crowns of <a href="Kingdom%20of%20Castille" Castille</a and <a href="Crown%20of%20Aragon" Aragon</a in 1479, the use of <a href="Spanish%20language" Spanish</a gradually became more prestigious and marked the start of the decline of Catalan. Starting in the 16th century, Catalan literature came under the influence of Spanish, and the urban and literary classes became <a href="bilingualism" bilingual</a.

With the <a href="Treaty%20of%20the%20Pyrenees" Treaty of the Pyrenees</a (1659), <a href="Spain" Spain</a ceded the <a href="Northern%20Catalonia" northern part of Catalonia</a to <a href="France" France</a, and soon thereafter the <a href="Northern%20Catalan" local Catalan varieties</a came under the influence of <a href="French%20language" French</a, which in 1700 became the sole official language of the region.
